sentences.text: Yes.
Actually, as far as I know, was the kapos, the Jewish police.
Schindler, I'm pretty sure-- there must be some kind of list of names from Schindler's factory.
However, the, the final list was drawn up in Krakau-Plaszow by some of the Jewish police with some of the people who actually were very close to Schindler.
Well, I don't know the name.
I wouldn't want to use any names.

But there were some people were very close working-- those, those are the people who would actually help him to, to get this factory-- the enamel pots and pans factory-- and were advisors to him-- how to handle things.
I was very short time.
I would say only a few days.
Because they were-- the Nazis were in a hurry to get as much, as much as possible-- get as many people, also, as possible-- to get out from Cracow.
Now, I was much shorter because I spent some time still in the factory where we disassembled the machinery.
Other people-- most of the people-- they're much longer.
And they were assigned to disassemble some of the barrack, to open up the mass graves because the Nazis wanted to eliminate all of the evidence.
So what they did-- they opened up the mass graves where the people were buried.
For some of them, recently.
Some of them, for two or three years.
And they were carrying the remains on a, on a certain spot where they put wood, and the, the remains were poured-- they, they were pouring gasoline over the remains, and they were burning to ashes.
They didn't want to leave any evidence of what was going-- or as little as possible-- what was going on.
So some of the people from Schindler's factory actually were assigned to this kind of work to clean up this camp.
And this was a horrible time.
I was lucky that we were working in Schindler's factory to disassemble the machinery and put on the train.
By the way, I will mention one other thing-- an incident.
What happened while I was there working to disassemble the machines.
One day, we-- what happened-- a plane was shot by the Nazis and split in half.
And half of the plane fell into the camp and the other half on the outside.
And while we were-- the, the plane fell in.
A fire started, and one of the barracks was on fire.
Luckily, this one was the barrack where the people already left to go to Krakau-Plaszow was empty.
Otherwise, the few people-- probably some people would be killed.
And we were-- we started to put out the fire.
And after we put out the fire, I noticed there was still a pilot sitting in a little chair.
And his head was burned off.
His arms were burned off.
However, on the arm, I noticed an insignia, which was written Australia.
And so we figured out that this must be one of the English planes which was shot down.
After a while, the Nazis came in, they chased us away, and they start investigating.
Four years ago, when I was in Poland with my wife and my son, we went to Schindler's factory, and I tried to get in.
They wouldn't let us in.
But the guard at the gate was talking to us.
And I told him about this incident.
He said, by the way, you know what?
Now I believe you that you were here.
Because-- come over with me.
He showed me on the side on the building, the Australian government erect a plaque with names of the pilots which were killed in this incident.
He said, here, every August, people from Australia-- they come and have a ceremony commemorating this incident.
And I didn't know about the plaque, but what a coincident that--
--this man show me that here is-- you, you are telling the truth.
So here's another thing what happened while we were there.
In the cattle cars.
This was, was really early fall, but still was quite hot.
The cattle cars were loaded with hundreds of people.
They gave us just a piece of bread and a bucket, and that's all what we had to-- if anybody needed to release himself, the bucket was for.
Smell-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very dirty and took a few days-- I don't remember how many days-- until we came to, to Gross-Rosen first.
No.
Closed.
Was completely closed.
And we were just sitting over there until, finally, it was opened when we arrived in Gross-Rosen.
It took only about two, two or three days, but it was terrible.
Very hungry.
Pardon?
No water.
There was one bucket.
How long, how long did it last?
Didn't last long.
So we were thirsty.
We were hungry.
But we made it.
Nobody, nobody, fortunately, died in the cattle car.
When the cattle cars were opened up in Gross-Rosen, then problems started, too.
Because those people-- this was another horrible concentration camp.
They came-- people-- the kapos over there, which were probably criminals.
And they treated us less than human.
They took us, first-- right away-- to a barrack.
They stripped us.
Whatever we had, we left in one spot.
They took us to another barrack.
They shaved us wherever hair grows.
And even-- so they inspected us completely.
To this extent, they even ordered us to turn around, and they looked into the rectum, if anybody by any chance is not smuggling some diamonds or whatever it is.
Can you imagine this kind of inspection?
And then we went to a shower room.
We left the shower room, and we went to another barrack.
And we were given some clothing-- the striped uniforms-- and we never got back to the belongings-- what we brought with us.
I still had with me a picture of my family.
And I tried to save-- that's the only thing I had with me.
I tried to save it.
And during this inspection, I kept it in my hand, and a SS or kapo ordered me to open my hand.
What do you have over there?
And I had, I had my picture of my family.
He said, open.
The picture dropped on the ground.
And this was all what, what I wanted to save.
Then I couldn't save this even.
Where we were, the conditions were terrible.
They kept us in the barrack-- in the small barrack-- they didn't give us those bed bunks.
They gave us-- we slept on the floor.
They give us just a little food.
Luckily, Schindler was working very hard to get us out from over there.
One day, they came.
They called our name.
They ordered us to get to the-- again, to the cattle cars.
And we were sent to Brunnlitz, Czechoslovakia, to Schindler's factory.
When we came-- again, the ride was only two or three days.
Again, without food.
The same condition.
Very terrible condition.
But when we came to the Schindler's factory, then we came, like, to heaven.
Because what Schindler did-- he took over a textile factory in Czechoslovakia.
Actually, this was very close to his home town where he originally came from.
We disassemble, disassemble the textile machinery and start reassemble the munition part-- the munition department.
Work was very hard because we had to unload the machines-- heavy machines-- from the railroad cars.
We had to bring it in.
We had to even build foundation because the heavy machinery couldn't stay on the floor like this.
But even work was very hard, nothing-- Schindler did everything possible to make conditions for us.
So the factory was on the first floor.
The second floor was our housing.
So actually we lived right in the, on the factory ground.
So when we went to work, we had to only go down from the second floor to the first floor.
We were already in factory.
They separated, again-- there was a very long building.
And they separate the building for women and men.
They were separated.
When the women finally came late in October of 1944-- came from Auschwitz-- that's-- we were there already.
And we start assembling the factory.
We-- again, don't forget this was almost by the end of the war.
The rations were very small.
Sometimes the Nazis-- the Germans-- couldn't get all the rations what they were assigned.
So, you know, they wouldn't feed the Jewish people.
But again, Schindler was going out-- knowing the territory well because he was from this area.
He was going out, buying food on the black market, bringing in to the camp, that we have enough, you know, to keep us alive.
Even once, he found out that there is a dead horse somewhere in the field.
He brought the horse in the camp, and we had some meat.
So whatever he could possibly do, he did.
One of the-- for example, as I mentioned to you before, in Plaszow and Gross-Rosen, we were so dirty that the lice were eating us alive.
We brought the lice into even Czechoslovakia.
And we were complaining to Schindler that something to be done about it.
So what Schindler ordered that on the upper floor there was-- where we were actually housed-- there were heating pipes going by the ceiling.
He ordered to build a small room and take all our clothing and put it into the room.
And the steam actually killed most of the lice.
And that's how he helped us out to get-- keep away from this problem.
One-- well, I don't-- I won't talk about the women part of it, because my wife was there.
Well, she, she will tell us.
She will tell you.
Well, when, when the women came to this camp, I will never forget the sight.
Because I don't think that we saw human beings-- creatures.
We thought, we are some creatures from the moon.
They were all shaven.
They came from Auschwitz without a hair.
They were-- in the movie they showed that they had some kind of head covering.
Nothing.
They were completely shaven.
We didn't recognize any of them.
Even to this extent-- clothing.
They were-- when they were given clothing, they were-- when they saw a big woman, they were given something small to just cover her body.
When they saw a little woman, they were given something big, like a coat or something.
It's, it's just to dehumanize, just to make sure that we don't look like human.
But after a while, you know, again, Schindler did everything possible to straighten things out a little bit.
And they were put to work.
And they were working in, in the factory.
Well, I will want to make sure that I will not get over this incident, which was not shown in the movie.
But I was even part of it.
In January of 1945-- winter 1945-- in Brunnlitz, Schindler find out that on the side tracks, there are some cattle cars with people in them.
He made his own business to go and see what's inside, what's going on.
And I was one of them.
He took few people.
We went by truck.
We tried to open those cattle cars.
We had very difficulty because the wheels of the main door of the cattle car doors were frozen.
We had to untie, somehow, just to get the cattle cars to open.
When we open, what we discover?
There were skeletons.
There were people in them.
Most of them were dead.
Some people still alive, and they kept themselves alive by eating the flesh of the dead people.
When we tried to remove those people from the cattle cars into the truck, we have difficulty.
Because they were sitting on the base on the, on the, on the car.
And they were frozen right into it because they were wet, no question, you know.
They were urinating and they were-- steam and whatever it is-- they were frozen.
So the, the clothing was frozen right to the-- so we tried to pull it.
The clothing was tearing.
Then when the clothing was tearing, then the flesh was tearing.
And you cannot imagine the sight-- what we, what we went through.
However, we did manage to get some people alive out.
Schindler took one of the barrack-- had one barrack-- he made a hospital out of it.
And at that time, Mrs.
Schindler was with him already.
And they both took care of the people which were taken from those cattle cars.
And they brought them alive.
They were feeding them slowly because you couldn't give them regular food.
So I saw myself, many times, Mrs.
Schindler walking through the courtyard with a pail of farina or some little soup in order to feed those people.
And some people survived the war.
Some people-- even before liberation-- some people, you could see them already walking around.
But also what other things Schindler did that-- he ordered to-- he arranged to bury the dead people on the cemetery with dignity.
So this was one other thing that Schindler did.
Well, in, in the factory, we worked over there.
We were also-- again, we were watched by SS people.
And we had a commandant by the name of Leopold.
He was another blood-thirsty man.
He was another man who liquidate many ghettos and concentration camps in Poland.
And he wanted-- he prepared for us a mass grave.
There was-- outside the camp was already prepared a mass grave for 1,000 people, to kill us.
Luckily, that he-- they ran out of time.
And again, through Schindler's manipulation, he tried to get Leopold away from the camp.
And he succeeded.
How?
By falsifying a document that Leopold has to report to Berlin on a certain day.
The, the document was typed up, and even-- we had one of the men in our camp who knew how to make a stamp-- a rubber stamp.
He falsified a rubber stamp.
The document was delivered to Leopold.
He left the camp and never had the opportunity to come back anymore because--
We found out-- most of the details we found out after the war, actually.
However, we knew that he, he was gone because Schindler said, if I can get rid of Leopold, I will manage with the other Nazis.
They were old men.
Don't forget, at that time, there was not too many young SS people.
They were just doing their business.
So he could manage to take care of the others.
In guard towers--
Not in the factory, no.
The foreman were German people, the German engineers and German professional people.
I had a foreman who was-- I worked directly under him.
And he was an old man-- very nice man.
He lived on the-- on the factory ground there was a building where he lived with his wife and a, and a child.
This man-- when he was coming to work, almost every day he brought something-- a piece of food for me-- in the pocket.
And he left on my bench.
He was not allowed to give it to me.
But he knew that I would steal it, and that's how he was helping me out a little bit.
Not to this extent.
But most of them were, were nice enough.
They didn't do-- they wanted you to do the job.
Because-- let me put this way-- in Czechoslovakia-- and many people are asking this question.
Because in the movie they showed that Schindler was sending some wrong, bad material out, delivered to the Nazis.
This was not the case.
Nobody would dare to sabotage the Nazis.
Because this was the number-one thing that they would execute this man right away.
But people don't realize that this was end of the war.
That when Schindler bought the factory, he had to get raw material.
He had to develop the process.
He had to get chemicals and all those things.
He did not have time to do-- to actually manufacture good parts.
So in order to satisfy the Nazis-- Nazis were after him.
You have 1,000 people.
You don't deliver anything.
What's the matter?
So what he used to do-- he used to go out to factory when we were making the same parts.
He used to buy the parts from other factories, brought to our factories.
And I saw myself, women sitting at the boxes and repacking it to his boxes.
And under his name, he delivered them to the authorities.
And he said, here it is.
Look.
My people are working.
We are producing.
And he-- that's where he spent a lot of his money-- what he made before-- in order to even satisfy the German authorities.
In, in Poland?
Well, mostly Polish people and few German.
They were-- they-- we didn't have any problem with them.
No.
They really-- they drive you to work.
They, they, you know, try to make you to work hard.
But nobody was really afraid of work.
We were afraid of guns and bullets.
Oh, yeah.
After the liberation, we went out--
No.
We didn't know.
However, after the liberation, also, we saw Schindler even prepared a wooden box with guns.
And some of his confidants knew about it, that he said, in case-- but we didn't know.
You see, there was no communication.
In case you're going to be taken to execution, you can protect yourself.
But we found out-- those things, I found out after the war.
During the war, however, we were cut off completely from the whole world.
But you know what Schindler did?
In his office, he kept his radio playing so loud, that some of the news-- people working around the office could hear.
And by word of mouth, they were spreading the news which way the war is going.
And we knew that the war is going toward, towards the end.
But the Nazis didn't let up till the last minute.
They were, they were trying-- and they were trying to the last minute-- to really finish us up.
Luckily-- again, because of Schindler-- we, we survived.
There was no guarantee, as I said before.
Because if the war would last longer, I don't think we could, could last also.
But the liberation came exactly the way you saw in the movie.
Schindler called us in, into the factory ground.
And he spoke to us.
He spoke to us like the father.
He warned us not to-- if the, when the liberation come, we're going to be free-- not to leave the camp, because it's unsafe.
There are still German soldiers and Nazis around, and they're shooting people.
Stay as long as possible.
And then, also, not to take law into your own hands.
Because he knew that there are some people what they didn't behave, behave themselves.
There were collaborators or what.
Don't take-- don't be like the Nazis.
Don't be like them.
Take-- when the authorities will come, turn it over to the authorities and let them take care of it.
We were there.
The liberation was exactly like you saw.
One Russian soldier on a horse came and told us, you are free.
You go wherever you want to go.
Go do whatever you want to do.
A few days-- a couple of days-- before the Russian came, came, the Nazis disappeared.
When Schindler left-- and he had to leave because, as you know, he was a Czech.
And the Czechs would be after him, and they probably would kill him.
They were afraid of the Russian.
The objective was to get him as soon as possible on the American zone in Germany and tell the story and hopefully, to save him and his wife.
And luckily, that's what had happened.
We were in this, in this camp for a while.
I was--
Yes.
Yes.
The night that--
He left with a, on a truck.
He took with him a truck and a Mercedes.
And there were a few people.
He was disguised in a striped uniform like the prisoners.
And few people from the camp went with him.
His wife was also disguised as a prisoner.
Just-- no.
Just two of them and few people from-- survivors from the-- prisoners went with him.
I think it was the next, next morning or the next day.
When the, the Polish soldier came up on horseback--
The Russian solder.
Russian.
There were some few people who spoke Russian.
And Russian and Polish is very similar.
So we could catch a few words here and there.
Because we all spoke Polish.
Well, then, I was in the camp for a while.
Then I went out one day after things quieted down and went to a farm.
I wanted to get rid of my striped uniform and get some clothing.
Went on a farm and asked a farmer if he could give me a pair of pants and a shirt, and he did.
And while I was there, I saw a chicken walking around.
I asked him, would you give me a chicken?
He said sure.
He caught the chicken and he gave me.
I brought the chicken to camp, and we killed it.
We cooked some chicken soup.
And this was the biggest mistake.
Because I-- after eating a little bit of chicken soup, I got sick.
My stomach couldn't take it.
And I was sick for a while.
And luckily, that wasn't serious.
And then, after a few weeks, the Russian authority came to the camp and said, announced, who wants to go back to Cracow-- to Poland?
We will establish a train, and you're going to be escorted.
We'll take you back to Cracow.
And we were-- I, I took the train.
I went by train to Cracow, came to Cracow.
And unfortunately, we were not welcome.
I went to my former neighbors where I lived.
And their only question was, are you still alive?
Well, we-- I-- what happened in Cracow-- Cracow was liberated in January of 1945.
A Jewish community center was established.
They took over a few buildings where before the war like was a, a, a, a Jewish orphanage.
And they established hotels for the people when they were coming back from concentration camp.
They gave everybody a cot and a blanket-- an army cot and a blanket.
And this was it.
And people were starting to come from camps, hidings, from other places.
And they joined together.
We were looking for some survivors.
I was hoping that I was going to find somebody from my family survived.
Unfortunately, nobody survived.
And in this house, that's where I met Helen, when she came back from Czechoslovakia to Cracow.
